messages.author: chixxxi
messages.text: @che aporeps 🌮 You might hate me for this, but I did it anyways. Please don't get mad, I am really only trying to figure out more details I am now running a custom build where I had cursor ai do an in depth analysis and implement fixes to avoid file system corruptions. I also had it analysis any potential fixes from MT. The changes, very briefly summarised are: Atomic writes + flush: DataStore (prefs/contacts/channels), IdentityStore, ClientACL now write to .tmp then rename(). Adv blob store: validate total file size on boot, recreate if wrong; putBlobByKey() write rate-limited (~200 ms); flush before close. nRF52/RAK4631: startup integrity checks for fixed-record files (contacts3 152B, channels2 68B); small delay(5) before rename() in IdentityStore. Deferred writes: new scheduler in MyMesh defers/coalesces prefs/channels saves (≈400 ms after radio/BLE idle, ≤2.5 s coalesce, 5 s hard deadline); added activity stamps and loop handling. Build/config: added LFS_THREADSAFE=1, LFS_BLOCK_CYCLES=128 (nRF52); included <Arduino.h> for delay(); I'll report within the next day on how this went. Thanks a lot for helping on this matter!!! I am a script kiddy (nowadays vibe coder), I have no chance for fixing this myself.
messages.content_html:
@che aporeps 🌮 You might hate me for this, but I did it anyways. Please don't get mad, I am really only trying to figure out more details I am now running a custom build where I had cursor ai do an in depth analysis and implement fixes to avoid file system corruptions. I also had it analysis any potential fixes from MT. The changes, very briefly summarised are:
  • Atomic writes + flush: DataStore (prefs/contacts/channels), IdentityStore, ClientACL now write to .tmp then rename().
  • Adv blob store: validate total file size on boot, recreate if wrong; putBlobByKey() write rate-limited (~200 ms); flush before close.
  • nRF52/RAK4631: startup integrity checks for fixed-record files (contacts3 152B, channels2 68B); small delay(5) before rename() in IdentityStore.
  • Deferred writes: new scheduler in MyMesh defers/coalesces prefs/channels saves (≈400 ms after radio/BLE idle, ≤2.5 s coalesce, 5 s hard deadline); added activity stamps and loop handling.
  • Build/config: added LFS_THREADSAFE=1, LFS_BLOCK_CYCLES=128 (nRF52); included <Arduino.h> for delay(); I'll report within the next day on how this went. Thanks a lot for helping on this matter!!! I am a script kiddy (nowadays vibe coder), I have no chance for fixing this myself.

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: fwiw, i've noticed corruption happening on a couple different t114v2s specifically around the time i plug in a charging cable. it occurred to me to wonder if we should have a voltage-level check before any write operation, as cisien mentioned that the nRF suffered from a marginal-voltage flash write problem. i suspect that there's a tiny blip in power when the charger is connected on the t114 (which may or may not be present on other boards), and if this occurs during a write, it could lead to corruption. i don't like it as a theory, because it's awfully coincidental that i'd have flash writes during the presumably small number of milliseconds a highly theoretical voltage dip would happen when plugging in the charging cable. but that's a pattern i noticed.
messages.content_html: fwiw, i've noticed corruption happening on a couple different t114v2s specifically around the time i plug in a charging cable. it occurred to me to wonder if we should have a voltage-level check before any write operation, as cisien mentioned that the nRF suffered from a marginal-voltage flash write problem. i suspect that there's a tiny blip in power when the charger is connected on the t114 (which may or may not be present on other boards), and if this occurs during a write, it could lead to corruption. i don't like it as a theory, because it's awfully coincidental that i'd have flash writes during the presumably small number of milliseconds a highly theoretical voltage dip would happen when plugging in the charging cable. but that's a pattern i noticed.

messages.reply_html:
Mike's Allotment
@che aporeps 🌮 I've been looking at some of the code today and spotted something that worries me. In IdentityStore:save()... #if defined(NRF52_PLATFORM) || defined(STM32_PLATFORM) _fs->remove(filename); File file = _fs->open(filename, FILE_O_WRITE); In LittleFS the remove function is autonomous and will delete the file. We then open the file to re-write its contents, however the actual write of data to the filesystem will not take place until close() is called - that's the 'benefit' of LittleFS. However, the initial remove call is autonomous - so the file will be removed before we then try to open for writing, write the data in memory, before then physically writing the data to the filesystem when the close() function is called. If there's a lot of data to write and save then there may be a long gap between the file being removed and the new file being written on close(). When you have a lot (300+) of contacts I'm guessing that could take a long time, so there's a decent window where you could end up without a file if you crash between removing the file and closing the new file. Surely it would be better to write the data to a temp file first, close that file, and then do a rename() as a single autonomous (and fast) call afterwards. Am i barking up the wrong tree here? (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I was confusing a filesystem block with a flash block, and assumed it was all 4K. Also before the extra storage was added the app used to show 28KiB (7*4096 ) now it shows 100KB so you obviously cannot tell any more if that other storage is full which was why I was confused by getting those errors. (edited)
messages.content_html: I was confusing a filesystem block with a flash block, and assumed it was all 4K. Also before the extra storage was added the app used to show 28KiB (7*4096 ) now it shows 100KB so you obviously cannot tell any more if that other storage is full which was why I was confused by getting those errors. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: If a file system block is smaller than a flash block, doesn't it lead to write amplification and slower performance? If you make them the same size don't you have almost exactly the same issue of a wasted 4K write for a smaller file. Unless the writes are buffered to fill a block, is that why there is a slight delay writing? (edited)
messages.content_html: If a file system block is smaller than a flash block, doesn't it lead to write amplification and slower performance? If you make them the same size don't you have almost exactly the same issue of a wasted 4K write for a smaller file. Unless the writes are buffered to fill a block, is that why there is a slight delay writing? (edited)

messages.author: intoccabile22
messages.text: What's the alternative? As testing showed, the FS requires resilience against outages and interrupts. So FAT is out of question per design. SPIFFS is worse than littlefs and deprecated. A full blown journaling file system is probably out of question due to complexity ? So far a copy-on-write FS with flash wear leveling does not sound to bad of concept - implementation may vary Genuinely interested what alternatives there are.
messages.content_html:
What's the alternative? As testing showed, the FS requires resilience against outages and interrupts.
  • So FAT is out of question per design.
  • SPIFFS is worse than littlefs and deprecated.
  • A full blown journaling file system is probably out of question due to complexity ? So far a copy-on-write FS with flash wear leveling does not sound to bad of concept - implementation may vary Genuinely interested what alternatives there are.
messages.reply_html:
che aporeps 🌮
after reading more about littleFS 2 i'm not sure it's great for us. the metadata overhead goes up significantly, which is ok for devices with external flash but probably not for devices that only have on-chip flash.

messages.author: intoccabile22
messages.text: Thanks. Just getting into this. So my assumption is that your customLFS for nrf52_base is forked from littlefs 1.6? However I don't yet understand whats the issue with littlefs 1.6 or 2.x are, and what challenges the customLFS solves. I just know that you put tremendous effort into making it work for meshcore.
messages.content_html: Thanks. Just getting into this. So my assumption is that your customLFS for nrf52_base is forked from littlefs 1.6? However I don't yet understand whats the issue with littlefs 1.6 or 2.x are, and what challenges the customLFS solves. I just know that you put tremendous effort into making it work for meshcore.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: intoccabile22

2

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I managed to see, and reproduce after, the "T1000 just dies" issue. It still had no corruption afterwards. The battery was at 3.3v when I deliberately started tried to run the battery down by using it, rebooting, connecting to BT and turning on GPS each time. It took a very long time to run down completely but I saw the battery go down to 3.09v / 8% with no issues then at some point while out of the room it shut off. In this state in unsurprisingly would not turn back on but immediately did so if connected to power. While I cannot be sure what other people are experiencing I'm pretty certain its just the battery going flat in most cases so I don't really understand what people expect it to do. It is also back up to 350 contacts but still not seeing any more of the "lfs warn:314: No more free space" errors accompanying the expected [SOC ] NRF_EVT_FLASH_OPERATION_SUCCESS. The Rak WiMesh Tag did the same thing and no corruption there. The behaviour is different though, it seems to shutdown just below 3v but after a while reboots and runs for while then shuts down again, which is worse that just staying off. (edited)
messages.content_html: I managed to see, and reproduce after, the "T1000 just dies" issue. It still had no corruption afterwards. The battery was at 3.3v when I deliberately started tried to run the battery down by using it, rebooting, connecting to BT and turning on GPS each time. It took a very long time to run down completely but I saw the battery go down to 3.09v / 8% with no issues then at some point while out of the room it shut off. In this state in unsurprisingly would not turn back on but immediately did so if connected to power. While I cannot be sure what other people are experiencing I'm pretty certain its just the battery going flat in most cases so I don't really understand what people expect it to do. It is also back up to 350 contacts but still not seeing any more of the "lfs warn:314: No more free space" errors accompanying the expected [SOC ] NRF_EVT_FLASH_OPERATION_SUCCESS. The Rak WiMesh Tag did the same thing and no corruption there. The behaviour is different though, it seems to shutdown just below 3v but after a while reboots and runs for while then shuts down again, which is worse that just staying off. (edited)
